agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO described it at a high level during an all-hands meeting, leaving most specifics undefined. This is a pre-specification concept, not a detailed requirements document.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The product name for the real-time dynamic delivery pricing system" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "A measure of delivery request volume relative to capacity in a given timeframe and/or geography — exact definition unspecified" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing — thresholds undefined" },
      { "term": "Base Price", "definition": "Implied but not stated: the standard delivery price before dynamic adjustments are applied" },
      { "term": "Price Adjustment", "definition": "The delta applied to a delivery price in response to demand signals — could be multiplier or additive, unspecified" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must be adjustable in real time in response to demand signals",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a business capability decision, not a data integrity constraint. A VP could decide to pause dynamic pricing and revert to static rates.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a change in demand level, the system produces an updated price within a defined real-time threshold (threshold undefined)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "When demand increases, price must increase relative to the base or previous price",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a pricing policy. The business could choose to hold prices flat during demand spikes (e.g., during a natural disaster for PR reasons). It is a policy, not a physical or data-integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand level D2 > D1, the computed price P2 >= P1 (monotonically non-decreasing with demand)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "When demand decreases, price must decrease relative to the base or previous price",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Same reasoning as BR-2 — this is a pricing policy that encodes competitive strategy, not a system integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand level D2 < D1, the computed price P2 <= P1 (monotonically non-increasing as demand falls)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The final delivery price must never fall below zero",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A negative delivery price means paying customers to take deliveries. This is a financial integrity constraint — the system is corrupt if it charges negative amounts. No reasonable policy change would make negative prices valid.",
      "testable_assertion": "For all computed prices P, P >= 0"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Price guardrails must enforce an upper bound on the dynamically adjusted price",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The CEO said 'probably' guardrails. Even if implemented, the ceiling value is a configurable business policy, not a hard system constraint. A VP could raise or lower the cap.",
      "testable_assertion": "For all computed prices P, P <= configured_price_ceiling"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Price guardrails must enforce a lower bound (price floor) on the dynamically adjusted price",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "A minimum price floor protects profitability but is a policy lever. The floor value could be adjusted by business leadership. Distinct from BR-4 (non-negativity) because the floor would typically be above zero to cover costs.",
      "testable_assertion": "For all computed prices P, P >= configured_price_floor where configured_price_floor >= 0"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The pricing system must operate fully automated without manual human intervention for individual price decisions",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation is a product design choice. The business could add manual override or approval workflows later. This is an operational policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "Price adjustments are computed and applied without requiring human approval in the critical path"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "A quoted delivery price, once accepted by a customer, must be honored at that price",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a contractual and financial integrity invariant. If the system could retroactively change an accepted price, it would corrupt order state and violate the customer contract. This is implied by the nature of pricing in commerce, even though the CEO did not state it.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a customer has accepted a quoted price P for a delivery, the charged amount equals P regardless of subsequent demand changes"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes to extreme levels (e.g., 100x normal) — holidays, weather events, system outages", "expected_behavior": "Guardrail price ceiling caps the price. Exact ceiling undefined — needs business decision." },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero in a region", "expected_behavior": "Price floor prevents pricing below cost. Exact floor undefined — needs business decision." },
    { "scenario": "Demand data feed becomes unavailable or stale", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Should the system fall back to base price? Freeze last known price? Halt quoting? This is a critical gap." },
    { "scenario": "Rapid oscillation in demand causes price flickering", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Customers could see price change between viewing and accepting. Needs dampening or quote-lock policy." },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price-gouging laws triggered during emergencies", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Some jurisdictions prohibit surge pricing during declared emergencies. Guardrails may need to be jurisdiction-aware." },
    { "scenario": "Two customers request the same delivery route milliseconds apart under changing demand", "expected_behavior": "Each should receive a price consistent with the demand level at their quote time. Race conditions must not produce inconsistent pricing." },
    { "scenario": "Price floor exceeds price ceiling due to misconfiguration", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. System must detect and reject contradictory guardrail configurations — this is an invariant gap." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Dynamic pricing directly affects revenue and margin. Correct guardrails are critical — too aggressive means customer churn, too conservative means leaving money on the table. Incorrect pricing (e.g., negative prices, uncapped surge) could cause material financial harm.",
    "operational": "Full automation removes manual pricing overhead but introduces dependency on demand signal accuracy and system reliability. Demand feed failures become a pricing outage.",
    "user": "Customers will perceive price fairness as a trust signal. Surge pricing without transparency risks brand damage. Price flickering degrades UX and trust. Quoted-price honoring is table stakes.",
    "risk": "HIGH — Regulatory risk from price-gouging laws in certain jurisdictions during emergencies. Reputational risk from perceived unfairness. Financial risk from guardrail misconfiguration or demand signal corruption.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Prices will fluctuate — transparency and fairness perception are critical to retention" },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue Team", "impact": "Directly affected by pricing accuracy, margin protection, and guardrail configuration" },
      { "group": "Operations/Dispatch", "impact": "Demand signals must be accurate; pricing may influence delivery volume patterns" },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review for price-gouging regulations, consumer protection laws, and contractual obligations" },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will field complaints about price variations — needs tooling to explain why a price was what it was" },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "If driver compensation is tied to delivery price, dynamic pricing directly affects their earnings" }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 25,
    "reasoning": "The spec is a casual CEO statement at an all-hands meeting, not a formal requirements document. Almost every critical detail is undefined: demand measurement methodology, pricing algorithm, guardrail thresholds, fallback behavior, quote validity windows, geographic scoping, regulatory considerations, and the relationship between price and cost. The word 'probably' before guardrails means even that concept is not confirmed. This is a vision statement, not a specification.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Prices adjust based on demand (high demand = higher price, low demand = lower price) — this is explicitly stated",
      "The system is intended to be automated",
      "Some form of guardrails is intended (though not confirmed)",
      "Price must never be negative (universal financial integrity constraint)"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "How demand is measured (volume? capacity ratio? geography? time window?)",
      "What the pricing algorithm is (linear? tiered? multiplier?)",
      "What the guardrail thresholds are (ceiling and floor values)",
      "Whether guardrails are even confirmed ('probably')",
      "Quote validity and price-lock behavior",
      "Fallback behavior when demand data is unavailable",
      "Regulatory compliance requirements by jurisdiction",
      "Whether courier/driver compensation is affected",
      "Whether pricing is per-route, per-zone, or global"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "There exists a base price that dynamic adjustments modify — not stated but implied",
      "Price honoring on accepted quotes is required — standard commerce practice, not stated",
      "Guardrails include both a ceiling and a floor — CEO only said 'guardrails' generically",
      "Demand is measured at some geographic and temporal granularity — completely unspecified",
      "The price-demand relationship is monotonic (higher demand never lowers price) — implied but not guaranteed"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Demand measurement is entirely undefined. Is it request volume per hour? Ratio of requests to available couriers? Per-zone or global? Time-windowed? Without this, the core pricing input cannot be implemented correctly.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Finance/Revenue Team", "Operations/Dispatch"],
      "options": ["Request volume per time window per zone", "Request-to-capacity ratio per zone", "Composite demand index incorporating multiple signals"],
      "recommendation": "Require Product to define 'demand' with a precise, measurable formula before any implementation begins"
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Guardrails are described with 'probably' — it is unclear if they are confirmed, and no thresholds (ceiling, floor) are specified. Without guardrails, the system could produce extreme prices.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Legal/Compliance", "Finance/Revenue Team"],
      "options": ["Mandatory guardrails with defined ceiling/floor", "Optional guardrails configurable per market", "No guardrails with monitoring and manual kill-switch"],
      "recommendation": "Guardrails should be mandatory with business-defined thresholds before launch. This is a legal and reputational necessity, not optional."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "No pricing algorithm is specified. The relationship between demand level and price adjustment is unknown — linear scaling, tiered brackets, exponential, multiplier-based?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Finance/Revenue Team", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Linear multiplier on base price", "Tiered demand brackets with fixed price offsets", "ML-driven pricing model"],
      "recommendation": "Start with a simple, auditable algorithm (tiered brackets or linear multiplier) that the business can reason about, before considering ML approaches"
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of regulatory or legal review. Dynamic/surge pricing is regulated in many jurisdictions, especially during declared emergencies. Price-gouging laws could expose the company to fines and litigation.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Executive Team", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Proceed without legal review", "Block launch until legal review per operating jurisdiction"],
      "recommendation": "Legal review is mandatory before launch. Add jurisdiction-aware guardrail overrides for declared emergencies."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No specification of fallback behavior when demand signals are unavailable, stale, or corrupted. This is a critical operational gap for a fully automated system.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Operations/Dispatch", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Fall back to base price", "Freeze last known dynamic price", "Halt quoting until signal restored"],
      "recommendation": "Fall back to base price with an alert to operations. Never halt quoting — that is a revenue outage."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of price transparency to customers. Customers seeing fluctuating prices without explanation will perceive unfairness. No specification of whether/how to communicate why a price is what it is.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Customer Support", "Product/UX"],
      "options": ["Show demand indicator to customers", "Show only final price with no explanation", "Show price breakdown including demand factor"],
      "recommendation": "At minimum, indicate demand level qualitatively (e.g., 'High demand in your area'). Full transparency builds trust."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
